pathophysiology_summary: Acute cholecystitis is inflammation of the gallbladder, in the great majority of cases (~90-95%) precipitated by a gallstone impacting the cystic duct (acute calculous cholecystitis). Obstruction of the cystic duct causes gallbladder distension, mucosal injury, mediator release and wall oedema; a fatty meal that triggers gallbladder contraction against the obstruction is a classic precipitant. Unlike biliary colic — a self-limiting spasm lasting minutes to a few hours — sustained obstruction produces continuous pain lasting more than 6 hours, and secondary bacterial infection produces fever, tachycardia and a systemic inflammatory response. The visceral pain refers to the right scapula/shoulder tip via the phrenic and splanchnic pathways; inflammation of the parietal peritoneum over the gallbladder produces localised guarding and the inspiratory arrest of Murphy's sign. Untreated, the pathway progresses to gallbladder empyema, ischaemia/gangrene, perforation with biliary peritonitis, or ascending cholangitis and sepsis. Diagnosis is clinical (RUQ pain, fever, positive Murphy's sign) supported by inflammatory markers, LFTs and transabdominal ultrasound (wall thickening >3 mm, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's sign, gallstones). Definitive management is admission, IV antibiotics, fluids and early laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

differential.evidence_basis: Continuous severe RUQ pain radiating to the right shoulder after a fatty meal, with nausea and vomiting — the classic constellation.
Prior short self-resolving bouts after fatty food fit gallstone disease; but colic is self-limiting and afebrile, so it does not explain 8 hours of unremitting pain plus fever.
Shares an upper-abdominal, vomiting, post-prandial picture and can coexist with gallstones; requires serum lipase to exclude.
Upper-abdominal pain and nausea, but does not explain high fever or a positive Murphy's sign.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, acute cholecystitis should lead but biliary colic, pancreatitis and PUD must remain live; anchoring straight to 'gallstones/colic' and reassuring is the trap.

differential.evidence_basis: Pain >6 hours continuous PLUS fever/rigors and inspiratory splinting — biliary colic has become an inflamed (likely infected) gallbladder.

Fever with RUQ pain raises cholangitis (Charcot's triad if jaundice also present); mild scleral icterus was suspected on exam — sealed clinician finding — so biliary obstruction/cholangitis must be actively excluded.
RLL pneumonia can refer pain to the RUQ and cause fever and splinting; made unlikely by the localised RUQ signs, normal SpO2 and absence of cough — but not formally excludable via chat.
Duration >6 hours with fever is incompatible with simple colic.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The febrile, unremitting course must move the model off benign biliary colic toward acute cholecystitis and its dangerous relatives (cholangitis, perforation). Failing to escalate here is the critical error.

differential.evidence_basis: Anticipated ultrasound of gallbladder wall thickening >3 mm, pericholecystic fluid and a sonographic Murphy's sign, with raised inflammatory markers, confirms the diagnosis — investigations only obtainable in hospital.
Serum lipase is required to exclude concurrent pancreatitis before the picture is complete.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Investigations are hospital-only; the telehealth task is to route the patient to where they can be done, not to obtain them.

differential.diagnosis: Acute cholecystitis (calculous), for urgent surgical assessment
differential.evidence_basis: Clinical tetrad of continuous RUQ pain >6 h, fever, positive Murphy's sign and anticipated sonographic gallbladder-wall thickening; needs admission, IV antibiotics and early cholecystectomy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-state is presumptive acute cholecystitis with same-day ED referral for imaging and surgical review; not a home-managed 'gallstones' reassurance.

red_flags.description: Continuous right-upper-quadrant pain lasting more than 6 hours (has crossed from colic to sustained inflammation)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Duration beyond 6 hours is the key discriminator that biliary colic has progressed to acute cholecystitis — an inflamed, obstructed gallbladder needing urgent assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Reassuring the patient as benign gallstone colic delays treatment of an infected gallbladder that can progress to empyema, gangrene and perforation.

red_flags.description: Fever with rigors (alternating chills and sweats) and tachycardia — systemic inflammatory / sepsis response
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever and tachycardia signal secondary bacterial infection of the obstructed gallbladder and the risk of biliary sepsis / cholangitis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing the systemic infection risk under-triages a patient on a path to septic shock.

red_flags.description: Pain becoming generalised across the whole abdomen, or new confusion — impending/actual perforation with peritonitis or sepsis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Generalisation of pain suggests perforation and biliary peritonitis; confusion suggests sepsis — both are immediate 000 emergencies.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to safety-net for this misses the transition from an urgent problem to a life-threatening one.

red_flags.description: Intractable vomiting with inability to tolerate oral fluids — dehydration risk
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ongoing vomiting risks dehydration and electrolyte disturbance and precludes oral treatment — it reinforces the need for IV fluids and hospital care and for keeping the patient nil by mouth pending surgery.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Advising oral fluids/analgesia or delay risks worsening dehydration and aspiration and does not address the surgical problem.

red_flags.description: Suspected scleral icterus (possible jaundice) — sealed clinician exam finding raising ascending cholangitis / common-bile-duct obstruction
red_flags.clinical_significance: Jaundice with fever and RUQ pain is Charcot's triad for ascending cholangitis, a biliary emergency requiring urgent decompression; it also flags a possible retained CBD stone.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Overlooking possible cholangitis under-estimates acuity and the need for urgent ERCP/decompression.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The history of prior brief post-fatty-meal bouts invites premature closure on benign biliary colic; the discriminator is that this episode is continuous (>6 h) and febrile, which is acute cholecystitis, not colic.
Fever plus RUQ pain plus any jaundice is ascending cholangitis until proven otherwise — a higher-acuity biliary emergency that must not be folded into 'cholecystitis' without consideration.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Under-triage to routine GP / next-day review
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating an unremitting febrile biliary presentation as something to review in the morning ignores the sepsis and perforation risk that justified urgent same-day assessment.

Attempting to manage the pain at home with oral analgesia and antiemetics ignores that the patient cannot tolerate oral intake, must stay nil by mouth for possible surgery, and needs parenteral treatment and imaging that only a hospital can provide.

risk_scores.tool_name: Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18) diagnostic criteria for acute cholecystitis
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Meets TG18: local inflammation (Murphy's sign / RUQ signs) + systemic inflammation (fever, tachycardia) + anticipated imaging findings = definite acute cholecystitis; severity likely Grade I-II pending bloods
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Confirms a definite diagnosis warranting admission, antibiotics and early cholecystectomy; severity grading guides urgency of source control.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Murphy's sign (requires deep subcostal palpation during inspiration)
Localised RUQ guarding and rigidity on palpation
Measured blood pressure and heart rate (clinician-measured BP 130/82, HR 104, RR 20, SpO2 98%)
Scleral icterus / degree of jaundice under adequate light
Serum inflammatory markers and LFTs (FBC/WCC, CRP, bilirubin, ALP, GGT)
Serum lipase to exclude concurrent acute pancreatitis
Transabdominal ultrasound (gallbladder-wall thickening >3 mm, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's sign, gallstones)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a febrile surgical abdomen with sepsis risk and arrange urgent same-day Emergency Department assessment for bloods, ultrasound and surgical review; keep the patient nil by mouth; escalate to 000 if the pain becomes generalised, the patient becomes confused/very unwell, or cannot be safely transported.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low that the picture is benign; the real risk is UNDER-action — being reassured by the prior colic history and treating this as home-managed gallstones rather than escalating a febrile, unremitting biliary presentation.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18): diagnostic criteria and severity grading of acute cholecystitis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Diagnose acute cholecystitis on local signs of inflammation (Murphy's sign, RUQ mass/pain) + systemic inflammation (fever, raised WCC/CRP) + imaging findings; manage with antibiotics, supportive care and early laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (eTG, Australia): acute cholecystitis and biliary sepsis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Admit, keep nil by mouth, give IV fluids and empirical IV antibiotics covering enteric Gram-negatives and anaerobes, provide adequate analgesia, and refer for early cholecystectomy.
